headline: Europe's springtime of nations, California gold, and a Chinese schoolteacher named Hong
summary: 1848 was the closest the nineteenth century came to a global political convulsion. Barricades rose in Palermo in January, Paris in February, Vienna, Berlin, Milan and Budapest in March; monarchies wobbled or fell across the continent, and Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels published The Communist Manifesto in London in late February. In the same year James Marshall's discovery of gold at Sutter's Mill drew tens of thousands of migrants to California; the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo transferred a third of Mexico to the United States; the Second Anglo-Sikh War extinguished the Sikh Empire in the Punjab; and in the Guangxi hills a failed civil-service candidate named Hong Xiuquan founded the God Worshipping Society that would become the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom. Almost nowhere on earth did the year pass quietly.

snapshots.text: The European revolutions of 1848 began in Sicily on 12 January, cascaded through the Italian states, and detonated in Paris on 22-24 February when a banned reform banquet became barricades and Louis Philippe abdicated; the Second Republic was proclaimed on 25 February with Alphonse de Lamartine at the Provisional Government. On 13 March Vienna rose, Chancellor Metternich resigned and fled, and Emperor Ferdinand I promised a constitution. On 15 March Budapest rose behind Lajos Kossuth and Sandor Petofi; on 18-19 March barricades in Berlin forced Frederick William IV to concede a Prussian assembly. The Frankfurt Parliament opened in the Paulskirche on 18 May to draft a constitution for a united Germany. In Milan the Cinque Giornate expelled the Austrians on 22 March; Charles Albert of Piedmont declared war on Vienna. On 21 February Marx and Engels published Manifest der Kommunistischen Partei in London. By summer the tide had turned: General Radetzky won Custoza on 25 July, General Windischgratz retook Prague and shelled Vienna in October, Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte was elected president of France on 10 December. Only in France did a republic survive the year, and even it would fall to a coup in 1851.

In the Americas 1848 was defined by war ending, gold beginning, and slavery contested. On 2 February the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ended the Mexican-American War: for fifteen million dollars the United States acquired what became California, Nevada, Utah, most of Arizona and New Mexico, and parts of Colorado and Wyoming, some 525,000 square miles. Nine days earlier, on 24 January, the carpenter James W. Marshall had noticed flakes of gold in the tailrace of a sawmill he was building for John Sutter on the American River near Coloma. President James K. Polk confirmed the discovery in a message to Congress on 5 December; by 1849 the world was pouring into California. On 19-20 July the Seneca Falls Convention in upstate New York, organised by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Lucretia Mott, issued the Declaration of Sentiments demanding women's suffrage. Zachary Taylor, hero of Buena Vista, defeated Lewis Cass and Martin Van Buren to become the twelfth president. In the Caribbean, France abolished slavery in its colonies on 27 April, freeing some 250,000 people in Guadeloupe, Martinique and Reunion. In Brazil the coffee economy of the Paraiba valley was booming on the labour of nearly two million enslaved people.

Qing China under the Daoguang Emperor was still absorbing the humiliations of the First Opium War of 1839-42 and the Treaty of Nanjing that had opened five ports, ceded Hong Kong to Britain, and imposed an indemnity. In 1848 opium continued to pour in through Guangzhou, Fuzhou, Xiamen, Ningbo and Shanghai. In the hill country of Guangxi province the failed civil-service candidate Hong Xiuquan, who had experienced visions after reading a Christian missionary tract, was preaching a heterodox monotheism together with his cousin Feng Yunshan and a charcoal-burner named Yang Xiuqing. Their Society of God Worshippers was gathering thousands of Hakka villagers, foreshadowing the Taiping Rebellion that would erupt in 1851 and kill perhaps twenty million people. In Japan the Tokugawa shogunate under Ieyoshi maintained sakoku, but American whalers were already probing Pacific waters and Commodore Matthew Perry was five years from Uraga Bay. In Vietnam the emperor Thieu Tri had died in 1847; his successor Tu Duc began a long reign that would end with French colonial conquest. In Korea Cheoljong reigned under Andong Kim regency.

The Punjab was the story of 1848 in South Asia. The First Anglo-Sikh War had ended in 1846 with a British protectorate; but on 18 April 1848 a revolt in Multan by Diwan Mulraj Chopra, in which two British officers were murdered, provided the pretext for a full annexationist war. Governor-General Lord Dalhousie mobilised the Army of the Punjab under Sir Hugh Gough; the sanguinary battles of Ramnagar in November, Chillianwala in January 1849 and Gujrat on 21 February 1849 broke the Khalsa Army. On 29 March 1849 the ten-year-old Maharaja Duleep Singh, son of Ranjit Singh, was compelled to surrender the Koh-i-Noor diamond and his empire; the Punjab was annexed to British India. In Bengal the East India Company under Dalhousie was extending railway surveys, and the first Anglo-Indian normal schools were opening in Calcutta. In Afghanistan the emir Dost Mohammad Khan, restored after the disastrous First Anglo-Afghan War, ruled from Kabul. In Iran Muhammad Shah Qajar died on 5 September; his sixteen-year-old son Naser al-Din, guided by the reforming chief minister Amir Kabir, ascended in October to what became a forty-eight-year reign.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Abdulmecid I was in the middle of the Tanzimat reforms proclaimed by the Gulhane Edict of 1839. In 1848 the Sultan gave asylum to Kossuth, the Polish revolutionary Jozef Bem and other refugees from the failed Hungarian revolution, defying Russian and Austrian demands for extradition and drawing British and French diplomatic support. In Egypt the aging Muhammad Ali Pasha, incapacitated by dementia, was formally deposed by his son Ibrahim on 20 July; Ibrahim ruled only four months before dying in November, whereupon Abbas Hilmi I succeeded. In the Levant the Druze and Maronite communities of Mount Lebanon lived in the uneasy peace that followed the 1840-41 confessional violence. In Algeria the war of French conquest continued: the emir Abd al-Qadir, who had surrendered in December 1847, was interned in France despite promises of exile to the Muslim east. In the Hijaz the sharifate of Mecca under Muhammad ibn Awn managed the pilgrimage under Ottoman oversight. In Persia the Bab, Sayyid Ali Muhammad Shirazi, publicly proclaimed his messianic claim; his followers would fight three regional wars against Qajar troops before his execution in 1850.

Southern Africa in 1848 saw the Cape Colony's frontier at war: the War of the Axe of 1846-47 against the Xhosa under Sandile had ended with the annexation of the Ciskei as British Kaffraria, and the new governor Sir Harry Smith proclaimed the Orange River Sovereignty on 3 February 1848, extending Cape authority north of the Orange over Boer trekkers and Griqua and Sotho communities. Andries Pretorius led Boer resistance; Smith defeated him at the battle of Boomplaats on 29 August, though within four years Britain would abandon the sovereignty and recognise the Boer republics. In Liberia, which had declared independence from the American Colonization Society on 26 July 1847, President Joseph Jenkins Roberts's new republic gained British recognition. The Sokoto Caliphate under Aliyu Babba ruled across the West African savanna; Umar Tall's Tijani jihad was gathering in the Senegambia. In Ethiopia the Zemene Mesafint dragged on with rival warlords vying at Gondar. The Atlantic slave trade to Brazil and Cuba was still huge, but the Royal Navy West Africa Squadron intercepted a growing number of slavers off the Bight of Benin. Explorers David Livingstone and William Cotton Oswell were preparing their 1849 expedition across the Kalahari.

The Australian colonies in 1848 were a decade past the end of convict transportation to New South Wales (1840) but still receiving convicts in Van Diemen's Land, where the settler campaign against transportation was gathering under the Anti-Transportation League. Pastoral expansion continued to consume Aboriginal land: massacres such as those in the Wide Bay district and the Darling Downs went largely unrecorded, though the German missionary Christopher Eipper and the Aborigines' Protection Society were beginning to document them. Ludwig Leichhardt's third expedition disappeared into the interior in April 1848, never to be found. In Melbourne, incorporated as a town in 1842, La Trobe governed the still-attached Port Phillip District that would become the colony of Victoria in 1851. In New Zealand the Wellington region was recovering from Governor George Grey's 1846-47 wars against Ngati Toa chief Te Rauparaha; the Constitution Act of 1846 had been suspended, and Grey ruled by ordinance while land purchases from Ngai Tahu and other iwi accelerated. Across Polynesia, whaling and missionary trade continued to reshape the island societies of Tahiti, Samoa, and Fiji.
